title: Computable Functors and Effective Interpretability
overall_summary: The paper proves that, for countable structures in computable relational languages, effective interpretability of A in B is equivalent to the existence of a computable functor from the isomorphism class of B to the isomorphism class of A. The nontrivial direction constructs, from a computable functor, a parameter-free Δ^c_1 interpretation of the target structure inside the source by coding target elements as finite pieces of source diagrams whose functorial transport is already witnessed by finite computations. The paper further proves an effective uniqueness result: the original functor and the functor reconstructed from the induced interpretation are effectively naturally isomorphic. It extends the equivalence to effective bi-interpretability, showing that two structures are effectively bi-interpretable exactly when they are computably bi-transformable by pseudo-inverse computable functors. For the original problem, the main value is the toolkit for translating between uniform computable behavior on all copies, automorphism transport, and computably infinitary Σ/Δ definability of relations or maps inside structures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Intrinsic computability versus computably infinitary definability, quoted from Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure, and let R be a relation on A^{<ω}. The relation R is uniformly relatively intrinsically computably enumerable, abbreviated u.r.i.c.e., if there is a c.e. operator W such that for every copy (B,R^B) of (A,R), the interpreted relation R^B equals W^{D(B)}, where D(B) is the atomic diagram of B. The relation R is uniformly relatively intrinsically computable, abbreviated u.r.i. computable, if there is a computable operator Ψ such that for every copy (B,R^B) of (A,R), R^B equals Ψ^{D(B)}. The paper recalls the theorem that R is u.r.i.c.e. if and only if R is definable without parameters by a computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formula; R is u.r.i. computable if and only if both R and its complement are definable without parameters by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas. For R⊆A^{<ω}, this means a computable list of Σ^c_1 formulas, one for each arity. A sequence of relations (R_i : i∈ω), with each R_i⊆A^{<ω}, is treated as a single relation on A^{<ω}×ω and is Σ^c_1-definable or u.r.i.c.e. in that uniform sense.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not reprove this characterization; it cites Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm, and Montalbán for the A^{<ω} version. Its role in the paper is foundational: whenever the authors build a relation uniformly by a c.e. or computable operator from atomic diagrams of copies, they can assert the existence of parameter-free computably infinitary Σ^c_1 or Δ^c_1 definitions, and conversely they can compute interpreted domains and relations from diagrams because their definitions are Δ^c_1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be countable structures in computable languages, assumed without loss of generality to be relational. Write Iso(A) for the category whose objects are copies of A with domain ω and whose morphisms are isomorphisms between such copies. A is effectively interpretable in B if there are parameter-free Δ^c_1-definable relations Dom^B_A⊆B^{<ω}, an equivalence relation ∼ on Dom^B_A, and relations R_i on powers of B^{<ω}, closed under ∼, such that the quotient (Dom^B_A/∼; R_0/∼,R_1/∼,...) is isomorphic to A. A computable functor from Iso(B) to Iso(A) is a functor F such that one Turing operator Φ computes the atomic diagram of F(B̂) from D(B̂), and another Turing operator Φ_* computes F(f) from D(B̂)⊕f⊕D(B̃) for every isomorphism f:B̂→B̃. The theorem states that A is effectively interpretable in B if and only if there exists a computable functor from Iso(B) to Iso(A).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward direction is Proposition 13: from a Δ^c_1 interpretation of A in B, each copy of B computes the interpreted quotient and hence a copy of A, and each isomorphism of copies of B acts coordinatewise on tuples and induces an isomorphism of the interpreted quotients. The reverse direction is Proposition 14: from a computable functor F=(Φ,Φ_*), one defines a domain of codes (finite tuples from B together with a natural number i) exactly when a finite identity-transport computation by Φ_* already fixes i; two such codes are equivalent if finite functorial transport computations map i to j and back. Relations of A are interpreted by asking for finite evidence in Φ that the corresponding atomic relation is true on equivalent representatives. Lemmas 15 through 22 verify that these definitions are Δ^c_1, that ∼ is an equivalence relation, that the interpreted relations are well-defined on equivalence classes, and that the quotient is isomorphic to A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A is effectively interpretable in B by parameter-free Δ^c_1 data Dom^B_A⊆B^{<ω}, an equivalence relation ∼, and relations R_i closed under ∼ whose quotient is isomorphic to A, then there exists a computable functor F:Iso(B)→Iso(A).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given a copy B̃ of B with domain ω, the Δ^c_1 definitions of Dom^B_A, ∼, and the R_i allow one to compute, uniformly from D(B̃), the corresponding interpreted domain Dom^{B̃}_A, equivalence relation, and quotient relations. Using a fixed enumeration of ω^{<ω}, choose uniformly a bijection τ^{B̃}:ω→Dom^{B̃}_A/∼, for instance by enumerating equivalence classes in their first occurrence order. Pull the quotient structure back along τ^{B̃} to obtain a structure F(B̃) on domain ω, which is a copy of A. If h:B̃→B̂ is an isomorphism, extend h coordinatewise to tuples in B̃^{<ω}; this maps Dom^{B̃}_A to Dom^{B̂}_A, respects ∼ and all R_i, and therefore induces an isomorphism of quotients. Define F(h)=(τ^{B̂})^{-1}∘h∘τ^{B̃}. The construction is uniform in D(B̃)⊕h⊕D(B̂), and functoriality follows immediately because coordinatewise action of isomorphisms on tuples preserves identities and compositions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose F=(Φ,Φ_*) is a computable functor from Iso(B) to Iso(A). Fix presentations of A and B on domain ω. For a finite tuple b̄ from B, let D(b̄) be the finite partial atomic diagram determined by b̄ under the paper’s fixed coding, and let λ be the identity map on ω. Define Dom^B_A to consist of pairs (b̄,i)∈B^{<ω}×ω such that the finite-oracle computation Φ_*^{D(b̄)⊕λ↾|b̄|⊕D(b̄)}(i) converges with value i. For (b̄,i),(c̄,j)∈Dom^B_A, define (b̄,i)∼(c̄,j) if there is a finite tuple d̄ of elements outside b̄ and c̄ such that, writing c̄′ for the elements of c̄ not in b̄ and b̄′ for the elements of b̄ not in c̄, and taking the finite permutation σ matching the tuple b̄ c̄′ d̄ with c̄ b̄′ d̄, the computations Φ_*^{D(b̄ c̄′ d̄)⊕σ⊕D(c̄ b̄′ d̄)}(i)=j and Φ_*^{D(c̄ b̄′ d̄)⊕σ^{-1}⊕D(b̄ c̄′ d̄)}(j)=i both converge. For each relation symbol P_m of A of arity p(m), define R_m on p(m)-tuples of Dom^B_A by putting ((b̄_1,k_1),...,(b̄_p,k_p)) in R_m if there are a finite tuple c̄ and numbers j_1,...,j_p such that each (b̄_s,k_s)∼(c̄,j_s) and the finite output Φ^{D(c̄)} already asserts P_m(j_1,...,j_p). Then Dom^B_A, ∼, and all R_m form a parameter-free Δ^c_1 effective interpretation of A in B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction uses finite pieces of the functor computations as codes for elements of the functor output. A pair (b̄,i) is admitted into the interpreted domain only when the functorial image of the finite identity map on b̄ already fixes the target element i; this ensures that i is independent of harmless extensions of b̄. Equivalence of two codes is witnessed by a finite partial isomorphism between finite diagrams of B whose functorial image maps i to j and whose inverse maps j to i. Lemma 15 shows Dom^B_A and its complement are Σ^c_1; Lemma 16 does the same for ∼ and its complement by defining a negative relation via finite computations with wrong output and using totality plus functoriality to prove exactly one of the positive and negative alternatives holds; Lemma 17 proves ∼ is an equivalence relation by composing finite witnesses and using functoriality. Relations R_m are first seen to be c.e. from the diagram; Lemma 20 defines the complementary relation Q_m by finite evidence that P_m is false and proves Q_m is exactly the complement of R_m, so each R_m is Δ^c_1. Corollary 21 gives closure of R_m under ∼. Finally, using the fixed copy B, map each i∈ω, viewed as an element of the fixed copy of A, to (B↾n,i), where n is least such that this pair lies in Dom^B_A. Lemmas 18 and 19 show this map is surjective and injective modulo ∼, and the definition of R_m shows it preserves all atomic relations. Hence the quotient is isomorphic to A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14 from a computable functor F=(Φ,Φ_*), the interpreted domain Dom^B_A={(b̄,i): Φ_*^{D(b̄)⊕λ↾|b̄|⊕D(b̄)}(i)=i} and its complement are both definable in B, without parameters, by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each finite tuple length and each i, enumerate all finite partial diagrams δ for which the finite computation Φ_*^{δ⊕λ↾k⊕δ}(i) converges with value i. Each such δ corresponds effectively to a quantifier-free formula describing the atomic facts of a tuple, and the computable disjunction of these formulas defines Dom^B_A. For the complement, enumerate finite partial diagrams δ for which the same finite-oracle computation either converges to a value different from i or demands information outside δ or outside the finite identity λ↾k. The paper uses the convention that this latter kind of finite-oracle failure is a detectable divergence from the finite oracle, not an infinite run. Because Φ_* is total on genuine full oracles D(B^f)⊕id⊕D(B^f), no actual tuple from a copy of B realizes a finite situation in which the computation remains forever undecided without asking beyond the finite oracle. Thus the complementary disjunction defines exactly the complement of Dom^B_A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14, the equivalence relation ∼ on Dom^B_A and its complement within Dom^B_A×Dom^B_A are both definable in B, without parameters, by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The relation ∼ is visibly Σ^c_1: one existentially guesses a finite tuple d̄ and checks the two finite computations by Φ_* witnessing transport of i to j and back. For the complement, define a Σ^c_1 relation ≁ by the same finite setup, but requiring that at least one of the two transport computations converge to a value different from the required one. The main point is to prove that for pairs in Dom^B_A, exactly one of ∼ and ≁ holds. To see at least one holds, extend the relevant finite tuples b̄ and c̄ to bijections f,g:ω→B that agree off a finite initial segment; then h=g^{-1}∘f is an isomorphism between the pullback copies B^f and B^g. Since Φ_* computes the total isomorphism F(h), the images of i and j are determined after finitely much oracle use, giving either a positive ∼ witness or a negative ≁ witness. To see both cannot hold, suppose finite witnesses give contradictory functorial transports. Choose full bijections extending the finite data for the positive and negative witnesses. These yield a diagram of isomorphisms among pullback copies of B. Functoriality says that the image under F of one composite is the composite of the images under F of the pieces. The domain condition for (b̄,i) and (c̄,j) supplies identity actions on the relevant coordinates, while the positive and negative finite witnesses force different values for the same functorial composite, a contradiction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 17
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14, the relation ∼ is an equivalence relation on Dom^B_A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Symmetry is built into the definition, since the witness includes both σ and σ^{-1}; reflexivity follows from the defining condition for membership in Dom^B_A, using the finite identity map. For transitivity, suppose (ā,i)∼(b̄,j) and (b̄,j)∼(c̄,k). Choose finite witnesses for both equivalences, then extend the relevant tuples to bijections f_1,f_2,g_1,g_2,h_1,h_2 from ω onto B arranged so that the finite partial isomorphisms witnessing the two equivalences and the identity behavior at b̄ occur as restrictions of full isomorphisms among pullback copies. Functoriality expresses F(h_2^{-1}∘h_1) as a composite passing through the first witness, the identity at b̄, and the second witness. Applying this composite to i gives k; applying the inverse composite to k gives i. Since these computations use only finitely much of the full oracles, a sufficiently long finite tuple supplies the required finite witness that (ā,i)∼(c̄,k).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14, if (b̄,i)∈Dom^B_A, then there is an initial segment c̄=B↾n of the fixed presentation B and some j∈ω such that (b̄,i)∼(c̄,j). Here B↾n denotes the tuple (0,1,...,n−1) in the fixed presentation of B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose n large enough that every element appearing in b̄ lies in B↾n. Choose a finite permutation σ of {0,...,n−1} sending the first |b̄| positions to the tuple b̄, and extend σ to a permutation f of ω that is the identity on all numbers at least n. The pullback B^f is isomorphic to B via f, and F(f) is computed by Φ_*. Let j=F(f)(i). Since the computation of F(f)(i)=j and the inverse computation use only finitely much oracle information, enlarge n to m if necessary so that B↾m contains all information used. The finite restriction then witnesses (b̄,i)∼(B↾m,j).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 19
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14, if (b̄,i),(c̄,j)∈Dom^B_A and b̄ is a subtuple or initial part of c̄ in the sense that the tuple c̄ extends b̄, then (b̄,i)∼(c̄,j) if and only if i=j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because (b̄,i) is in Dom^B_A, the finite identity computation Φ_*^{D(b̄)⊕λ↾|b̄|⊕D(b̄)}(i) converges to i. Any finite witness deciding whether (b̄,i) is equivalent or non-equivalent to (c̄,j) extends this same identity computation on the part corresponding to b̄. Therefore the target value forced by the computation is i. By Lemma 16, exactly one of equivalence and non-equivalence holds; hence equivalence holds precisely when j equals i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 20 and Corollary 21
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a relation symbol P of A of arity p in the construction of Proposition 14, and let R be the interpreted relation defined by finite positive evidence that P holds in Φ^{D(c̄)} on suitable ∼-equivalent representatives. Define Q analogously by requiring finite evidence that P does not hold in Φ^{D(c̄)}. Lemma 20 states that Q is exactly the complement of R inside (Dom^B_A)^p. Consequently each interpreted relation R_m is Δ^c_1-definable, uniformly in m. Corollary 21 states that if (b̄_s,i_s)∼(c̄_s,j_s) for s=1,...,p, then ((b̄_1,i_1),...,(b̄_p,i_p)) lies in R if and only if ((c̄_1,j_1),...,(c̄_p,j_p)) lies in R; that is, R is closed under ∼ coordinatewise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: To prove every tuple is in R or Q, use Lemma 18 to replace each coordinate by an equivalent code based on a sufficiently long common initial segment c̄=B↾n. The full output Φ^{D(B)} is a copy of A, so it decides whether P holds on the resulting tuple of indices. Enlarging c̄ to cover the finite use of that decision gives either a positive R witness or a negative Q witness; Lemma 19 ensures that passing to the longer initial segment does not change the indices. To prove R and Q are disjoint, suppose positive evidence is witnessed by c̄,j_1,...,j_p and negative evidence by d̄,k_1,...,k_p. By transitivity of ∼, each (c̄,j_s) is equivalent to (d̄,k_s). Extend c̄ and d̄ to bijections f,g:ω→B. Then F(g^{-1}∘f) is an isomorphism from F(B^f) to F(B^g), and the equivalences force it to send each j_s to k_s. But the finite Φ-output over c̄ makes P(j_1,...,j_p) true in F(B^f), while the finite Φ-output over d̄ makes P(k_1,...,k_p) false in F(B^g), contradicting preservation of relations by the isomorphism F(g^{-1}∘f). Closure under ∼ follows immediately from this disjointness argument and the definition of R.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 22
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 14, define θ:ω→Dom^B_A by θ(i)=(c̄,i), where c̄=B↾n is the least initial segment of the fixed presentation B such that (c̄,i)∈Dom^B_A. Then θ induces an isomorphism between the fixed copy A=(ω;P^A_0,P^A_1,...) and the interpreted quotient (Dom^B_A/∼;R_0/∼,R_1/∼,...).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Injectivity modulo ∼ follows from Lemma 19: two codes based on comparable initial segments with different second coordinates cannot be ∼-equivalent. Surjectivity modulo ∼ follows from Lemma 18, which says every domain code is equivalent to one whose first coordinate is an initial segment of B; then Lemma 19 allows replacement by the least such initial segment. Preservation and reflection of each relation P_m follow directly from the definition of R_m, because the R_m relation was defined by looking for representatives over a common tuple on which Φ has already output the corresponding atomic fact. Thus θ gives the required quotient isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) be a computable functor. Apply Proposition 14 to F to obtain an effective interpretation of A in B, and then apply Proposition 13 to that interpretation to obtain a computable functor I_F:Iso(B)→Iso(A). Then F and I_F are effectively naturally isomorphic. That is, there is a computable Turing functional Λ such that for every copy B̃ of B, Λ^{B̃}:F(B̃)→I_F(B̃) is an isomorphism, and for every isomorphism h:B̃→B̂, the naturality equation I_F(h)∘Λ^{B̃}=Λ^{B̂}∘F(h) holds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each copy B̃, Proposition 14 supplies a computable map F_{B̃}:F(B̃)→Dom^{B̃}_A/∼ as in Lemma 22, sending each element i of F(B̃) to its interpreted code. Proposition 13 constructs I_F(B̃) by choosing a computable bijection τ^{B̃}:ω→Dom^{B̃}_A/∼ and pulling the quotient back to ω. Define Λ^{B̃}=(τ^{B̃})^{-1}∘F_{B̃}. This is uniformly computable from D(B̃). To verify naturality, let h:B̃→B̂ be an isomorphism and suppose F(h)(i)=j. Let F_{B̃}(i)=(ā,i) and F_{B̂}(j)=(b̄,j), after possibly extending the finite tuples so that the computation of F(h)(i)=j is already witnessed by finite oracle information. Since h sends the finite diagram of ā in B̃ to the finite diagram of h(ā) in B̂, the definition of ∼ in B̂ gives (h(ā),i)∼(b̄,j). This is exactly the assertion that the square comparing F(h), I_F(h), and the Λ maps commutes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 23
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be computable structures. Suppose A is effectively interpretable in B with interpreted domain Dom^B_A, and B is effectively interpretable in A with interpreted domain Dom^A_B. For any map q on a structure, write q̃ for the induced coordinatewise map on tuples and interpreted domains, and q̃̃ for the second iterate. Then the following are equivalent. (1) A and B are effectively bi-interpretable using the given interpretations, meaning that for some isomorphisms f^B_A:Dom^B_A→A and f^A_B:Dom^A_B→B, the compositions f^A_B∘f̃^B_A:Dom^{(Dom^B_A)}_B→B and f^B_A∘f̃^A_B:Dom^{(Dom^A_B)}_A→A are u.r.i. computable in B and A respectively. (2) There are u.r.i. computable isomorphisms g:Dom^{(Dom^A_B)}_A→A and h:Dom^{(Dom^B_A)}_B→B, together with isomorphisms α:Dom^B_A→A and β:Dom^A_B→B, such that α∘h̃∘α̃̃^{-1}=g and β∘g̃∘β̃̃^{-1}=h. (3) There are u.r.i. computable isomorphisms g and h as in (2) such that the same two displayed identities hold for every choice of isomorphisms α:Dom^B_A→A and β:Dom^A_B→B. Moreover, in (2) and (3), one may take g and h to be the u.r.i. computable composition maps arising from a bi-interpretation in (1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1) implies (2), take g=f^B_A∘f̃^A_B and h=f^A_B∘f̃^B_A, and take α=f^B_A and β=f^A_B. The displayed identities reduce to cancellations of a map with its induced inverse. For (2) implies (3), fix arbitrary α′:Dom^B_A→A and β′:Dom^A_B→B. Let δ be the automorphism of Dom^B_A with α′∘δ=α. Using h, extend δ to an automorphism γ=h∘δ̃∘h^{-1} of B. Since h is u.r.i. computable, γ fixes the graph of h, giving γ∘h∘γ̃̃^{-1}=h and hence δ∘h̃∘δ̃̃^{-1}=h̃ once one proves γ̃=δ. To prove γ̃=δ, the paper uses the u.r.i. computability of the corresponding transported version of g inside B: automorphisms preserve the graph of g, and this forces the induced actions to agree. Therefore replacing α by α′ leaves α∘h̃∘α̃̃^{-1} unchanged; the same argument with roles reversed handles β. For (3) implies (1), fix any isomorphism f^A_B:Dom^A_B→B and define f^B_A=g∘(f̃^A_B)^{-1}. Then f^B_A∘f̃^A_B=g is u.r.i. computable, and condition (3) gives f^A_B∘f̃^B_A=h, also u.r.i. computable. Hence the original interpretations form an effective bi-interpretation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A and B be countable structures. They are effectively bi-interpretable if and only if they are computably bi-transformable. Here computably bi-transformable means that there are computable functors F:Iso(A)→Iso(B) and G:Iso(B)→Iso(A) such that G∘F is effectively naturally isomorphic to the identity functor on Iso(A), F∘G is effectively naturally isomorphic to the identity functor on Iso(B), and the two natural isomorphisms are coherent in the paper’s pseudo-inverse sense: applying F to the natural isomorphism Ã→G(F(Ã)) gives exactly the natural isomorphism F(Ã)→F(G(F(Ã))), and similarly with A and B interchanged.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume first that A and B are effectively bi-interpretable. Proposition 13 gives computable functors F:Iso(A)→Iso(B) and G:Iso(B)→Iso(A). In the construction of F, there is a uniformly computable map Ω^{Ã}:Dom^{Ã}_B→F(Ã) identifying the interpretation of B inside Ã with the produced copy F(Ã); similarly Γ^{B̃}:Dom^{B̃}_A→G(B̃). The u.r.i. computable composition map from the bi-interpretation gives a computable functional Θ^{Ã}:Ã→Dom^{(Dom^{Ã}_B)}_A. Define Λ^{Ã}_A=Γ^{F(Ã)}∘Ω̃^{Ã}∘Θ^{Ã}:Ã→G(F(Ã)). This is an isomorphism uniformly computable from Ã. Naturality follows from three identities: F(j)=Ω^{Â}∘j̃∘(Ω^{Ã})^{-1} for an isomorphism j:Ã→Â; G(F(j))=Γ^{F(Â)}∘F(j)̃∘(Γ^{F(Ã)})^{-1}; and Θ is u.r.i. computable, so j̃̃∘Θ^{Ã}=Θ^{Â}∘j. The analogous construction gives Λ_B for F∘G. Proposition 23 then supplies the coherence equations required for pseudo-inverses. Conversely, assume pseudo-inverse computable functors F and G are given. Proposition 14 gives effective interpretations in both directions, and Proposition 7 provides the comparison maps Ω and Γ between the original functors and the interpretation-induced functors. From the natural isomorphism Λ_A:Ã→G(F(Ã)), define Θ^{Ã}=(Ω̃^{Ã})^{-1}∘(Γ^{F(Ã)})^{-1}∘Λ^{Ã}_A, viewed as a map Ã→Dom^{(Dom^{Ã}_B)}_A. Naturality implies that for every isomorphism j:A→Ã, the graph of Θ^{Ã} is the image under j of the graph of Θ^A; in particular Θ^A is fixed by automorphisms and the same infinitary definition works in every copy. Since Θ is also computed uniformly by a Turing functional, Θ^A is u.r.i. computable. Build Υ for B analogously. The pseudo-inverse coherence equations imply the identities in Proposition 23(2), so the two interpretations are an effective bi-interpretation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C and D be classes of countable structures on domain ω, viewed as categories whose morphisms are permutations of ω inducing isomorphisms and which are closed under such isomorphisms. The class C is reducible via effective bi-interpretability to D if for every C∈C there is D∈D such that C and D are effectively bi-interpretable and the formulas defining the interpretations and the composition isomorphisms are uniform, independent of the particular C and D. The class C is uniformly transformally reducible to D if there is a subclass D′⊆D and computable functors F:C→D′ and G:D′→C that are pseudo-inverses. Theorem 12 states that C is reducible via effective bi-interpretability to D if and only if C is uniformly transformally reducible to D.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a uniformization of Theorem 9. Every step in the construction from interpretations to functors in Proposition 13 is uniform in the defining formulas, and every step in the construction from functors to interpretations in Proposition 14 is uniform in the Turing operators Φ and Φ_*. Likewise, the passage between bi-interpretations and pseudo-inverse functors in Theorem 9 uses only uniform functionals and uniform definitions. Therefore a classwise family of effective bi-interpretations with fixed formulas yields pseudo-inverse computable functors on the whole class, and pseudo-inverse computable functors on a class yield a uniform family of effective bi-interpretations.

proof_techniques.name: Turning an effective interpretation into a computable functor by canonical quotient enumeration
proof_techniques.description: Given a Δ^c_1 interpretation of A in B, the paper computes the interpreted domain, equivalence relation, and relations inside each copy B̃ from D(B̃). It then chooses a canonical enumeration of the quotient Dom^{B̃}_A/∼ using a fixed enumeration of ω^{<ω}, and pulls the quotient structure back to domain ω. An isomorphism h between copies of B acts coordinatewise on tuples, hence on the interpreted quotient, and after conjugating by the chosen enumerations gives the functorial image F(h).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a precise way to transfer automorphisms through an interpretation: an automorphism of a copy of B induces, computably from the diagram and the automorphism, an automorphism of the interpreted copy of A. It is a natural tool for testing whether computable AUT-countability or definability of automorphism graphs survives an interpretation.

proof_techniques.name: Recovering definable structure from finite uses of a computable functor
proof_techniques.description: The key reverse construction codes an element i of the functor output F(B^f) by a finite tuple b̄ from B when the finite identity-oracle computation already proves that i is fixed. Two codes are identified when a finite partial isomorphism between finite diagrams of B has, through Φ_*, already transported i to j and j back to i. Atomic relations are interpreted by finite evidence from Φ that the output structure satisfies the relevant atomic fact on equivalent representatives.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can organize algorithms computing copies, isomorphisms, or automorphism actions into a computable functor, this technique turns that uniform computability into Σ^c_1 or Δ^c_1 definability inside the original structure. This is directly relevant to arguments that try to derive definability of automorphisms from uniform relative computability, or to identify where non-functoriality is needed to separate those notions.

proof_techniques.name: Defining complements by wrong-output finite witnesses and excluding overlap by functoriality
proof_techniques.description: For both the equivalence relation and the interpreted atomic relations, the paper first defines a positive Σ^c_1 relation by finite successful computations. It then defines a negative Σ^c_1 relation by finite computations that give an incompatible output or finite evidence of the negated atomic fact. Totality of the functorial operators on full copies guarantees that every genuine tuple eventually receives either a positive or negative finite witness, and functoriality of F excludes simultaneous positive and negative witnesses.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a reusable pattern for upgrading c.e. definability to Δ^c_1 definability in settings involving automorphisms: define positive and negative finite computation witnesses, then use preservation under composition or group action to prove consistency. It may help in showing that certain automorphism graphs are intrinsically computable or, conversely, in locating obstructions.

proof_techniques.name: Initial-segment representatives for finite-tuple codes
proof_techniques.description: Lemmas 18 and 19 show that every finite-tuple code is equivalent to a code whose tuple is an initial segment of the fixed presentation B, and that if one code’s tuple extends another, equivalence collapses to equality of the natural-number coordinate. This supplies canonical representatives and makes the final quotient isomorphism explicit: i maps to the least initial-segment code (B↾n,i) that belongs to the domain.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When coding automorphisms or elements by finite information from a presentation, this technique can eliminate dependence on arbitrary finite tuples and replace them by canonical initial-segment data. That can be useful when comparing definability relative to parameters with computability relative to the atomic diagram.

proof_techniques.name: Effective natural isomorphism as uniqueness of the functor induced by an interpretation
proof_techniques.description: After converting a computable functor F into an interpretation and then converting the interpretation back into a functor I_F, Proposition 7 constructs a computable natural isomorphism between F and I_F. The natural isomorphism is built from the canonical map between F(B̃) and the interpreted quotient inside B̃, followed by the quotient enumeration used to define I_F(B̃). Naturality is verified using the finite equivalence witnesses from the reverse construction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This shows that the coding choices in an effective interpretation do not change automorphism actions except by computable conjugacy. For a problem about whether automorphisms are computable or Σ-definable, this gives a way to move between different effective presentations of the same interpreted content while controlling the induced automorphisms.

proof_techniques.name: Pseudo-inverse computable functors as a replacement for effective bi-interpretations
proof_techniques.description: The paper’s bi-interpretability theorem replaces syntactic back-and-forth interpretations with computable functors F and G whose composites are effectively naturally isomorphic to the identity in a coherent way. The proof constructs the natural isomorphisms from the u.r.i. computable composition maps of a bi-interpretation, and conversely reconstructs those composition maps from the natural isomorphisms of pseudo-inverse functors.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides a route to transfer structural properties through computable bi-transformations rather than directly manipulating formulas. If an example for the original problem is easier to build in an effectively bi-interpretable coding, this theorem suggests how to track automorphisms and definability through the coding.

proof_techniques.name: Using u.r.i. computability as the bridge between algorithms on copies and infinitary definitions
proof_techniques.description: Throughout the paper, relations computed uniformly from every copy’s atomic diagram are treated interchangeably with parameter-free computably infinitary Δ^c_1 definitions. This is not merely a convenience: the proof of Proposition 14 repeatedly constructs relations by finite oracle computations and then invokes the intrinsic computability/definability correspondence to obtain formulas.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks about Σ^{in}_1-definability relative to finite parameters. A natural adaptation is to expand the language by constants for the chosen parameters and then ask whether the relevant automorphism graph becomes u.r.i.c.e. or u.r.i. computable in the expanded structure; this paper supplies the algorithm-to-definition mechanism in the parameter-free case.

proof_techniques.name: Uniformizing interpretations and functors over classes
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 12 observes that all constructions in Theorem 9 are uniform, so a classwise effective bi-interpretation with fixed formulas is equivalent to pseudo-inverse computable functors between the corresponding categories of structures. This allows reductions not just between individual structures but between entire classes, preserving the same effective interpretation scheme.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate structure is obtained by coding another structure into graphs, orders, lattices, fields, or another universal class, this uniform version indicates how to keep the coding effective and functorial across all copies. That can matter for cone-style hypotheses, where behavior must be controlled uniformly over presentations.

proof_techniques.name: Relationalization and finite-tuple domains
proof_techniques.description: The paper assumes all languages are computable and says that, without loss of generality, they may be taken relational. It also allows interpreted domains to be subsets of B^{<ω}, not just B^n for fixed n, and notes that finite objects and even relations on B^{<ω}×ω can be effectively coded by relations on B^{<ω}. This gives enough room to represent arbitrary finite computational data inside the structure.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem allows constants, functions, and relations. This paper’s setup suggests first replacing functions by their graph relations and using finite-tuple domains to encode auxiliary computational data, so that the effective-interpretability machinery can be applied without changing the essential automorphism behavior.

other_useful_info: The introduction states that effective interpretability is equivalent to the parameterless version of Ershov-style Σ-definability over HF(B), with a reference to Montalbán [Mon12, Section 4]. A footnote explains that requiring the interpreted domain to be Δ^c_1 rather than merely Σ^c_1 is no loss: if a Σ-domain is given by formulas ∃s̄ φ_i(x̄,s̄), replace the domain by tuples (x̄,s̄,i) satisfying φ_i and adjust the equivalence relation to identify tuples whose x̄-components were equivalent in the original interpretation. The introduction also cites [Mon, Lemma 5.3] for the fact that effectively bi-interpretable structures share many computability-theoretic invariants: degree spectrum, computable dimension, Scott rank, index set up to Turing equivalence for infinite structures, computable categoricity, rigidity, the c.e. extendability condition, relational degree spectra, and effective bi-interpretability of jumps. The paper notes that undirected graphs, partial orderings, and lattices are universal for effective bi-interpretability using constructions from Hirschfeldt-Khoussainov-Shore-Slinko; with finitely many constants added, integral domains, commutative semigroups, and 2-step nilpotent groups are also universal; and fields are universal by work of Miller, Park, Poonen, Schoutens, and Shlapentokh. All of these are only reported as background or consequences, not proved in detail in this paper.
why_relevant: Equates computable functorial reductions with effective interpretations. These tools help transfer computability of automorphisms, definability of relations, and cone behavior between coded structures.
